content:
Let $ (M, \mathcal{T}) $ be a topological Hausdorff space with a countable basis). Then $ M $ is called a topological manifold if there exists an $ m \in \mathbb{Z}^+ $ such that for each point $ p \in M $ we have an open neighborhood $ U $ of $ p $, an open subset $ V $ of $ \mathbb{R}^m $ and a homeomorphism $ x \colon U \to V $. The pair $ (U,x) $ is called a local chart (or local coordinates ) on $ M $. The integer $ m $ is called the dimension of $ M $ . To denote that the dimension of $ M $ is $ m $ we write $ M^m $.

From this definition, pay close attention to the definition of the so called local charts. These objects let us map our possibly weird topological space into the very familiar Euclidean space, point by point. We put this into a graphic for emphasis:

Topological manifolds are locally Euclidean.

But why is this important? Our above observation allows us to do some calculus on our space (since we know how to do calculus in Euclidean space). To this end, recall from the moral of differentiation that differentiation is a local property: the only thing that matters to differentiation is a small neighborhood around your chosen point. Combining this with morals our boxed observation, we get to the pleasant conclusion that

You can do calculus on manifolds.

We’ll get how to exactly do this new calculus, but at the least we now (or soon will) have the tools of calculus at our disposal. :D

References & Related Readings

  • An Introduction to Riemannian Geometry by Sigmundur Gudmundsson.
  • My dad.

The pair $ (U,x) $ is called a local chart (or local coordinates ) on $ M $. The integer $ m $ is called the dimension of $ M $ . To denote that the dimension of $ M $ is $ m $ we write $ M^m $. From this definition, pay close attention to the definition of the so called local charts. These objects let us map our possibly weird topological space into the very familiar Euclidean space, point by point. We put this into a graphic for emphasis:
Topological manifolds are locally Euclidean.
But why is this important? Our above observation allows us to do some calculus on our space (since we know how to do calculus in Euclidean space). To this end, recall from the moral of differentiation that differentiation is a local property: the only thing that matters to differentiation is a small neighborhood around your chosen point. Combining this with morals our boxed observation, we get to the pleasant conclusion that
You can do calculus on manifolds.
We’ll get how to exactly do this new calculus, but at the least we now (or soon will) have the tools of calculus at our disposal. :D
References & Related Readings An Introduction to Riemannian Geometry by Sigmundur Gudmundsson. My dad.
title: Introduction to Manifolds

content:
The spiel

Differentiation is first introduced via tangent lines to graphs that may or may not continue to good approximations after your chosen point. For example, $ f(x) = x^2 $ and its tangent line at $ x=1 $ is great at, say, $ x = 1 \pm 1/100 $ but is not so great at $ x = 1 + 100 $. Although a bit extreme, we get the following important idea from this: our tangent line may not represent the graph well after a certain point. :( In other words:

Differentiation is a local property. i.e., it only properly models your function near your point of interest.


For the next idea, consider the following function: $$ f(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & x \geq 0,\\ -1 & x < 0. \end{cases} $$
Here, although our function is seemingly very simple, there is a big problem at $ x=0 $—there’s no tangent line! So we can’t differentiate it. Leading us to your next idea:

Not every function admits a derivative. i.e., sometimes there’s no tangent line.


For our last point, consider the following seemingly very complicated function: $$ f(x) = e^{\sin x} + \cos (x^2). $$ This seems incredibly awful to work with, in my opinion, but let’s consider its derivative: $ f’(x) = \cos(x) e^{\sin x} - 2x \sin (x^2)$. Although still ugly, studying this function near, say, $ x = 50 $ is simple enough! We compute its derivative, $ f’(50) $ and consider our tangent line and now begin studying the function in that small neighborhood. Now we’ve simplified $ e^{\sin x} + \cos (x^2) $ to $ mx + b $ for some $ m $ and $ b $. This straight line is much, much easier to work with, yielding our final big idea:

Differentiation makes things easier to work with. It makes them more familiar.

Summary

We summarize with a pretty graphic:

  1. Differentiation is a local property.
  2. Differentiation isn’t always possible.
  3. When differentiation is possible, it simplifies things.

References & Related Readings

  • Just trust me lol.

title: The Moral of Differentiation

content:
We define idling as acting without purpose.
One must never idle (i.e., everything must be done with a purpose).

This is a corollary of

.

It’s okay (and beneficial) to take breaks and to recuperate and to enjoy oneself outside of work. It’s fine to turn one’s brain off! But it has to be done with reason! You must perform one of these inactions with purpose to relax, for example. Don’t turn your brain off and do nothing (or even something) if you have no purpose behind it.

.

You must perform one of these inactions with purpose to relax, for example. Don’t turn your brain off and do nothing (or even something) if you have no purpose behind it.
title: On Acting With or Without Purpose

content:
One must never resign.

If one resigns to their situation (i.e., gives up and no longer puts in effort into their life),

tells us that their life can no longer neither markedly improve and stay improved. Therefore we are violating Axiom #4 and thus one cannot resign.

Notice that there is a hidden "for all" quantifier in the above proposition (i.e., "For all instances in time, one must not resign, or, in other words, "There does not exist a time where one resigns"). This leads us to the following very important corollary (and its corresponding definition).
plain:
One must never resign. If one resigns to their situation (i.e., gives up and no longer puts in effort into their life),
tells us that their life can no longer neither markedly improve and stay improved. Therefore we are violating Axiom #4 and thus one cannot resign.
Notice that there is a hidden "for all" quantifier in the above proposition (i.e., "For all instances in time, one must not resign, or, in other words, "There does not exist a time where one resigns"). This leads us to the following very important corollary (and its corresponding definition).
title: On Resignation

content:
My word must have worth and, if it does not, I can ensure it will.
I have made many empty promises to myself in the past (whether I was aware or not). Each time I became aware of a such a lie I doubt my ability to change, to improve, to continue. This must not be possible. My word must have worth so that I can keep promises to myself (and to others).

We suppose without loss of generality that my word does not have worth. (As if it did we would be done!) In this case, what can be done to amend it? Like

states, this can only improve if I make a continuous effort, hence I will suppose my word does and act to make this true.

In some sense, it is irrelevant whether your word means anything as you must act like it does to give it the hoped for worth. Roughly speaking, this is “fake it till you make it”.

title: On the Worth of My Word

content:
My life can only markedly improve and stay improved only if I, myself, make an effort.

If my life did improve according to random chance it is not necessarily permanent. Such a random change may indeed be, once again, randomly taken away. If I am to make a sustained, positive change in my life, I must be able to control whether this change continues to take place. I must be the one deciding whether to continue instituting this change. Hence I must decide. Therefore, I must make an effort.

Someone else may help you in this process, or even start it (say a friend urging you to go the doctor or therapy), but you must make the final say in whether anything happens at all. You are always the crux of the attempt, though someone else may be a central aspect or instigator of it.
plain:
My life can only markedly improve and stay improved only if I, myself, make an effort. If my life did improve according to random chance it is not necessarily permanent. Such a random change may indeed be, once again, randomly taken away. If I am to make a sustained, positive change in my life, I must be able to control whether this change continues to take place. I must be the one deciding whether to continue instituting this change. Hence I must decide. Therefore, I must make an effort.
Someone else may help you in this process, or even start it (say a friend urging you to go the doctor or therapy), but you must make the final say in whether anything happens at all. You are always the crux of the attempt, though someone else may be a central aspect or instigator of it.
title: On Improving My Life

Pretty.
I like the edges of the gray clouds a lot. They seem out of place? But in such a way that they serve only to better the view. :)
A blob holds an axe! I wonder what they are slashing.
I really like the mix of colors around the sun in these remaining photos. I thought it looked super cool! :DDD

I liked the tree and stuff.
I honestly have no recollection of where this is? Maybe near frat row?

Wilshire and the urban hellscape that is Los Angeles has some modicum of beauty among its development, for once. /rolls-eyes

Non-descript sky.
Beautiful trees out of YRL with some pretty clouds too.
This stick looked like tha number 4. HUGE W.
More pretty trees and clouds.
Even more.
This isn’t really a cloud photo but I thought it looked really cool.
The clouds look like the river-carved surface of a moon or something. Crazy world.
Fluffy.
This and the next two photos are my favorite photos that I’ve taken this week. It’s beautiful how many different light levels there are and how it looks layered with some immense height, and with the bright sun stuck squarely in the middle. Beautiful stuff. Beautiful skies. I am so glad I looked up. :)
title: Week 2

content:
I close my eyes often.

Beyond blinking I choose to close them to imagine what has been, is, and can be.

I used to close my eyes and wish to open them to a new world,

To see the light of hope in another life,

To see all of this through eyes which were not my own.

Recently when I close my eyes I imagine what can be — what I can be.

I don’t wish for another life.

I see was mine can be made better.

I open my eyes.
plain:
I close my eyes often. Beyond blinking I choose to close them to imagine what has been, is, and can be.
I used to close my eyes and wish to open them to a new world,
To see the light of hope in another life,
To see all of this through eyes which were not my own.
Recently when I close my eyes I imagine what can be — what I can be.
I don’t wish for another life.
I see was mine can be made better.
I open my eyes.
title: Closing My Eyes

plain:
I took this photo when walking with home with my good friend Matt. :) I think that was the last time I will see him at UCLA as he’s going to Caltech now for his teaching/post-doc job.
I took the the remaining photos while walking from my friend Jacob’s performance of “The Waitress”! It was amazing. One of my absolute favorite plays that I’ve seen. :) He did a fantastic job!
I thought the cloud looked cool even though I feel the building detracts from the photo. :/
I like trees and clouds so this was a wonderful photo (albeit a bit blurry).
title: Week 1

plain:
Westwood Boulevard might have blocked some of the sky but what remained was pretty darn nice. :)
This reminds me of my high school fish-bowl sky photo! On the corner of Gayley and Lindbrook.
Those monolithic, empheral, white mountains above the construction site brings some much needed beauty lol.
These IFOs (Identified Flying Objects) are quite convincing! (They have been identified to be the lights in my friend’s apartment.)
title: May 2023

content: Here are my favorite clouds from my high school years.
plain:
Here are my favorite clouds from my high school years.
I took this photo before my zero-period P.E. class in my sophomore year of high school. It was my favorite photo that I’d ever taken at that time. :)
God’s white udders fill the sky with its blue milk.
I can’t identify this color because I’m incredibly colorblind but it still looks really cool.
It looked like I was in a fish bowl when I took this photo. Weird vibes.
A big warm blanket coddles me and prepares me for the day ahead.
I wonder what trailblazers left these here.
title: Pre-College

content:
The mountain is immovable.

It stands strongly despite all it endures.

Its clothes may be ragged but it continues to do what it must.

May I be a mountain.

May my shirt have holes.
plain:
The mountain is immovable.
It stands strongly despite all it endures.
Its clothes may be ragged but it continues to do what it must.
May I be a mountain.
May my shirt have holes.
title: Of Mountains and Shirts

content:
I am unburdened.

I am unburdened not because I have surmounted the seemingly-insurmountable, but because I have forgotten the weight I bear. It seems impossible that I could forget to carry my life, my responsibilities, my hopes, my dreams, but with a lack of clarity, things that may seem at the forefront of your mind may disappear into this all-too-powerful fog. I lost focus.

I forget.

I forget what I enjoyed and what I despised. I forget what I loved and what I feared. I have lost my identity, my sense of personhood. I am nothing but now what I am not. I am nothing.

How can I be burdened if there is nothing I hold dear, if there is no responsibility, if there is no passion?

Before I realize that I am in this enchanting fog, my world rolls off my shoulders and into the cold dark of the cosmos — I have shrugged without knowing. There is nothing quite so painful as realizing you have not done all that you could.

It falls further.

The abyss beckons me and calls out, “Follow your world. You are not fit to maintain it. You are not fit to maintain yourself.” Without a moment’s thought, I agree.

I feel the weight of my world on my shoulders.

How could this be possible? My world had disappeared before my eyes and I did nothing to slow its descent. Under this immense stress I realize: this is not the weight of my reality, but instead of what could have been. How do we bear the weight of an entire reality unrealized? How can I forgive myself for what I have not become?

I dive.

I begin to act on what is, burdened by the weight of what could have been, to attain what can be. I jump into the abyss’s dark embrace and race to reach my world. As I get closer, I hear a familiar voice reaching out to me: “Continue following your world. You are not fit to maintain it.” Without a moment’s thought, I disagree.

I reach.

I grasp.

I hoist.

I carry.

I feel the weight of my world on my shoulders.

title: Atlas: The Dive

content:
I crumple.

The world, my world, is the weight I bear. My responsibilities, my decisions good and bad, my hopes and dreams, everything I consider myself to be—the product of my very own existence—is held up by me. I strain trying to stay upright but my ankles falter. I nearly fall but I catch myself. As the world begins to slip out of my hands I tighten my grip and attempt to stand but my knees buckle and the greatest, tallest stand I can take is an imperfect squat—I am unbalanced. Unbalanced by the weight of what has been, what is, and what can be and what I associate with each of them. I begin to think the weight of my world is too much to bear. I fantasize of the relief I would feel if I were to stop.

I begin to yield.

Moments before I enter the cold and the dark of the cosmos, I think—seemingly on my own for the first time in what feels an eternity. Thoughts race across my mind: “Has it all been for naught?”; “Is my life nothing but a memory I will soon forget?”; “What am I becoming?” In this moment before relief, in this moment before disaster, I harken back to my final thought. What have I become? What did I do to come to where I am? Am I simply a product of my circumstances or the mistakes I’ve made along the way? Most importantly, what will become of me if I proceed? In this moment of clarity I lunge forward and grasp at my essence and all its adornments. I grab ahold and my knuckles begin to turn white. I don’t know if I have what it takes. I fear that I don’t. I long for what may happen if I do.

I assume a squat.

I begin thinking of what I may become now that I know I have what it takes. Now that I know the brink of oblivion and its fatalistic attraction is not enough to lure me in, I feel strong.

I rise.

I stop theorizing of what may happen or what I may do—I begin to do. I am unburdened.

I stand.

title: Atlas

content:
The mountains rest.

They sleep peacefully, happily, unstirringly night and day.

They rest as the wind blows and bellows across.

The trees leave them be.

They dig their roots into their skin,

into their flesh,

but they don’t mind.

The trees,

in becoming part of them,

turn into their clothes.

The imperfect canopy reveals their rocky skin.

Their shirts have holes.
plain:
The mountains rest.
They sleep peacefully, happily, unstirringly night and day.
They rest as the wind blows and bellows across.
The trees leave them be. They dig their roots into their skin,
into their flesh,
but they don’t mind.
The trees,
in becoming part of them,
turn into their clothes.
The imperfect canopy reveals their rocky skin.
Their shirts have holes.
title: The Mountains' Shirts Have Holes

content:
The wind whistles and howls,

It runs by the surface of our hills

terrorizing the grass below.

In their desperation,

Attempting to avoid devastation,

They dodge.

Back and forth they sway,

side to side.

They say,

They yell, “We will not relent!

“The misers may be strong but we are more!”

They continue to further themselves.

Spreading their roots,

Planting themselves firmly into their histories,

They grow stronger,

The wind blows.

From afar they dance.
plain:
The wind whistles and howls,
It runs by the surface of our hills terrorizing the grass below. In their desperation, Attempting to avoid devastation,
They dodge. Back and forth they sway, side to side. They say, They yell, “We will not relent!
“The misers may be strong but we are more!” They continue to further themselves. Spreading their roots, Planting themselves firmly into their histories, They grow stronger, The wind blows. From afar they dance.
title: The Mountains Dance

content:
The tempest erupts once again and everything is re-enlivened.

The beings of the old calibrate to the changing times, their roots rearrange and change, their leaves vibrate in the unrelenting winds, while their trunks stand strong in the force of powers greater than itself.

The tree that guards its underlings curls over to these winds. He forces the direction of the curl to move in a way such that his children are protected.

Even in the most tumultuous of tempests, he will cover his subordinates and continue on for another day.

The storm subsides.
plain:
The tempest erupts once again and everything is re-enlivened. The beings of the old calibrate to the changing times, their roots rearrange and change, their leaves vibrate in the unrelenting winds, while their trunks stand strong in the force of powers greater than itself.
The tree that guards its underlings curls over to these winds. He forces the direction of the curl to move in a way such that his children are protected.
Even in the most tumultuous of tempests, he will cover his subordinates and continue on for another day.
The storm subsides.
title: The Tempest

content:
He rises.

The knights, peacefully resting, wait for their next beckoning. They hold their breath as their king rises over the foothills, inching towards his kingdom’s highest point.

In his divine righteousness, he spreads rays of golden hope, promising a new day free from the strains of the last. Invigorated, his knights turn to bundles of fire and flames, transforming into a perfect inferno of red and orange. Their enchanting aggression lays siege to the blue plains, expanding the territory in which their kingdom lies.

Time passes and their king stands high and mighty. Now at his strongest, the king and his words transform the once ember-like knights burn bright white.

He reaches the kingdom’s apex.
plain: He rises. The knights, peacefully resting, wait for their next beckoning. They hold their breath as their king rises over the foothills, inching towards his kingdom’s highest point. In his divine righteousness, he spreads rays of golden hope, promising a new day free from the strains of the last. Invigorated, his knights turn to bundles of fire and flames, transforming into a perfect inferno of red and orange. Their enchanting aggression lays siege to the blue plains, expanding the territory in which their kingdom lies. Time passes and their king stands high and mighty. Now at his strongest, the king and his words transform the once ember-like knights burn bright white. He reaches the kingdom’s apex.
title: Dawn to Noon

content:
I feel the damp, soft grass push lightly against my back as I watch the puffy clouds transform above. I stare and I see a beautiful, fluffy, poorly drawn rabbit. He doesn’t seem to move much.

The wind bellows and leaves ruffle around me as my homely rabbit morphs into something a little more exciting. Fangs erupt from his mouth, his body elongates and curves, his button tail is grabbed and pulled by a gust, while his back is blown into curved wings. My beautiful bunny rabbit has morphed into a big, strong dragon.

The wind bellows and leaves ruffle around me as my homely rabbit morphs into something a little more exciting. Fangs erupt from his mouth, his body elongates and curves, his button tail is grabbed and pulled by a gust, while his back is blown into curved wings. My beautiful bunny rabbit has morphed into a big, strong dragon.
title: Cloud Watching
